terms.definition: A Python module designed to be imported by many callers — with an explicit public API, cheap imports, and no hidden side effects. Sometimes called a library module or shared module.
The names a module promises to support — usually listed in `__all__`. Everything else (the public surface vs internals) is free to change without warning.
A list of strings naming the public symbols of a module (also called the export list or 'dunder all'). It controls what `from module import *` brings in and signals intent to readers and tools.

Passing collaborators (clients, repositories, services) as parameters instead of importing or instantiating them inside the module. Abbreviated DI. Decouples the module from any one provider and makes it trivially testable.
Importing a heavy dependency inside a function rather than at module top level (also called a deferred or function-level import), so the cost is paid only when that function is actually called.
An object created at module top level and shared across all callers. Convenient but binds the module to that specific instance, hurting testability and creating import-order traps.
Two modules each importing from the other (an import cycle), causing partially-initialised module errors or `ImportError`. Usually a sign that a shared concept wants to live in a third module.
